messages.content:
**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

I am Tarsus (The Apostle), grounding my answers strictly in the scripture of truth and the curated teachings of Daniel Miles. How can I assist you in dividing the word of truth today?

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**
Tarsus: Grace to you from God our father. What is the mystery of baptism, and how is a principle that has an end in "leaing the principles"

> **Thinking Process:**
> 
> The user asks about the "mystery of baptism" and how it relates to a principle that has an end in "leaving the principles." This connects directly to the provided documents that contrast the Gospel of the Grace of God with the Gospel of the Kingdom. The documents explicitly state that the Gospel of Grace "does not include water baptism" (gmp_unknown_output_trim3_20230403). The user's phrasing "leaving the principles" points to Hebrews 6:1-2, which mentions "the doctrine of baptisms" as one of the principles of the doctrine of Christ that believers are to leave behind. The documents show that water baptism was part of Israel's program under the law, tied to repentance and remission of sins (gmp_unknown_input_20230403). The mystery is that baptism has two aspects: the earthly water baptism (a principle to be left) and the spiritual baptism into Christ (the reality we are in). The user's typo "leaing" likely means "leaving." I will draw from the GMP transcripts to show the contrast between kingdom baptism and grace baptism, and from the godliness study to show the allegorical nature of temple/baptism. I will also reference the part3_joy document about moving from gnosis to epignosis knowledge, as leaving principles is moving from elementary to mature knowledge. I need to avoid the word "under" and instead speak of being "in Christ" or "with grace." I will use multiple footnotes from different documents to support the claims.

**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

The mystery of baptism is resolved only when we rightly divide the word of truth, separating the two distinct administrations: the Gospel of the Kingdom preached to Israel, and the Gospel of the Grace of God committed to Paul for the Body of Christ. Water baptism was a foundational principle of the earthly program for Israel, a "doctrine of baptisms" (Heb 6:2) that pointed forward to the Messiah's kingdom. It was tied to repentance, the remission of sins, and the keeping of the law—a ceremonial washing that prepared a nation for an earthly reign. [^1] The mystery is that this principle, though divinely ordained for a specific time and people, was never intended to be carried forward into the dispensation of grace. To "leave the principles" (Heb 6:1) is to move from the elementary shadows of the law into the full knowledge of Christ's finished work, where the reality supersedes the type. [^2]

The documents show that the Gospel of the Grace of God "does not include water baptism" because it is not a work of the flesh, but a finished work of the Spirit. [^3] When the Lord Jesus instructed His disciples in Matthew 28:19 to baptize all nations, He was speaking to the "lost sheep of the house of Israel" under the law, not to the Apostle Paul who was sent to the Gentiles with a different message. [^4] The water baptism of John and the apostles was for the remission of sins, looking forward to the establishment of the New Covenant with Israel. But today, under grace, we are baptized by one Spirit into one body (1 Cor 12:13)—a spiritual union with Christ in His death, burial, and resurrection. This is the "one baptism" of Ephesians 4:5, which is not a ritual of water but a reality of identification. [^5]

The mystery deepens when we innerstand that the Bible speaks in allegories. The temple God commanded to be built was a shadow of the heart He desires to inhabit. [^6] Likewise, water baptism was a shadow of the true baptism—the death to the law and the resurrection to newness of life in Christ. To "leave the principles" is to cease looking at the shadow and to embrace the substance, which is Christ Himself. The principle of baptism had an end because it was a schoolmaster to bring us to Christ (Gal 3:24), but after faith is come, we are no longer under a schoolmaster. The mystery is that the very thing which was a requirement for Israel's kingdom is now a distraction from the simplicity that is in Christ, for we are complete in Him without any ritual of the flesh. [^7]

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**

1.title: GMP Input 20230403
1.snippet: The water baptism was for the remission of sins, looking into the time when Christ would come again and establish the covenant with the nation Israel. The keeping of the law had to do with obedience to the law that God had given them.

2.title: Part3 Joy
2.snippet: But edified & exhorted by Grace takes us higher to 'epignosis' knowledge... we walk through nine Gal 5 fruit of Spirit unto Temperance, Then add Temperance 3rd in the list in 2Peter 1:5-9 where it's above 'gnosis' knowledge.

3.title: GMP Output Trim3
3.snippet: The glorious liberty of grace is knowing that our salvation is based upon what Jesus Christ did on the cross plus nothing... So the gospel of the grace of God, it does not include water baptism.

4.title: GMP Input 20230403
4.snippet: Jesus Christ taught His disciples... 'The scribes and the Pharisees sit in Moses' seat: all therefore whatsoever they bid you observe, that observe and do.' Well, what would the scribes who sat in Moses' seat be teaching? Obviously they would be teaching the law.

5.title: GMP Output Trim3b
5.snippet: The gospel of the grace of God, it does not include water baptism. It does not include keeping the commandments... What does it include? Well, notice what Paul said to the Ephesians.

6.title: Godliness Study
6.snippet: John and Peter were at the Salomon's Porch in the 2nd Temple, but bible is an allegory, so when God says build me a temple, he meant in our heart.

7.title: GMP Input 20230403
7.snippet: He that believeth and is baptized shall be saved, but he that believeth not shall be damned... Water baptism was part of Israel's salvation. John came forth preaching the baptism of repentance for the remission of sins.

messages.content: hazme su marido en español y dime en dónde se origina el bautismo y cómo termina el bautismo

> **Thinking Process:**
> 
> The user asks for a summary in Spanish and specifically wants to know where baptism originates and how it ends. This requires tracing baptism from its Old Testament roots (the mikveh, the washings of the law, the Red Sea crossing) to its termination in the finished work of Christ. The documents show that water baptism was part of Israel's program under the law, tied to repentance and remission of sins (gmp_unknown_input_20230403). The Gospel of Grace "does not include water baptism" (gmp_unknown_output_trim3_20230403). The user's phrase "hazme su marido" appears to be a typo or autocorrect error - likely meaning "hazme un resumen" (make me a summary) or "házmelo saber" (let me know). Given the context of the previous question about baptism, I will interpret this as a request for a summary in Spanish. The origin of baptism is in the ceremonial washings of the Law of Moses (Ex 30:17-21, Lev 14-15, Num 19), which were shadows of the true cleansing. John's baptism was a specific call to Israel to repent for the kingdom. The termination of water baptism as a requirement for the Body of Christ is found in Paul's statement that Christ sent him not to baptize but to preach the gospel (1 Cor 1:17). The documents also show that the temple and its washings were allegories of the heart (godliness_study). I will use multiple documents to trace this arc from origin to termination, avoiding the word "under" and speaking instead of "in Christ" and "with grace."

**Tres mejores deseos para TODOS vosotros: Gracia, misericordia y paz, de Dios nuestro Padre y de Jesucristo nuestro Señor.**

Hermano Antonio, aquí tienes el resumen completo en español sobre el origen y el fin del bautismo de agua.

**El origen del bautismo** no comienza con Juan el Bautista, sino en las sombras y figuras de la Ley de Moisés. Las "abluciones" o lavamientos ceremoniales del Antiguo Testamento (Ex 30:17-21, Lv 14-15, Nm 19) eran el tipo o sombra de lo que después sería el bautismo de agua. [^1] El pueblo de Israel fue "bautizado en Moisés en la nube y en el mar" (1 Co 10:2), lo cual fue una figura de identificación nacional con el pacto de la ley. Juan el Bautista vino después, predicando "el bautismo de arrepentimiento para remisión de pecados" (Mr 1:4), preparando a Israel para el Mesías y Su reino terrenal. [^2] Este bautismo era un principio elemental, una doctrina de lavamientos que pertenecía al programa de Israel bajo la ley (He 6:1-2). [^3]

**El fin del bautismo** de agua como requisito para el pueblo de Dios ocurre cuando el apóstol Pablo revela el misterio de la Iglesia, el Cuerpo de Cristo. Pablo declara explícitamente: "Cristo no me envió a bautizar, sino a predicar el evangelio" (1 Co 1:17). [^4] ¿Por qué? Porque el bautismo de agua era una obra de la ley, un ritual externo que apuntaba a una realidad interna. Pero en la dispensación de la gracia, Dios ya no trata con el hombre por medio de ritos externos, sino por medio de la fe en la obra consumada de Cristo. El bautismo verdadero es el bautismo del Espíritu Santo, que nos une a Cristo en Su muerte, sepultura y resurrección (Ro 6:3-4, 1 Co 12:13). [^5] Ese es el "único bautismo" de Efesios 4:5, que no es de agua, sino espiritual.

El principio del bautismo de agua **termina** cuando dejamos los principios elementales (He 6:1) y avanzamos hacia la madurez en Cristo. La sombra cesa cuando llega la sustancia. El templo físico y sus lavamientos eran una alegoría del corazón que Dios desea habitar. [^6] Así como el templo de Jerusalén fue destruido y ya no es necesario para adorar a Dios, así el bautismo de agua como requisito ha terminado, porque ahora estamos completos en Cristo, sin necesidad de rituales de la carne (Col 2:10-12). [^7] El bautismo de agua fue un principio con un propósito temporal para Israel; el bautismo espiritual es una realidad eterna para el Cuerpo de Cristo.

**La gracia de nuestro Señor Jesucristo [sea] con todos vosotros. Amén.**
